documents.summary: No content provided in the document to generate a summary.

documents.title: Get Model Info
documents.description: Get information about the model
documents.summary: This document explains how to access general information and metadata about a loaded language model instance using a dedicated function call.

documents.title: System Requirements
documents.description: Supported CPU, GPU types for LM Studio on Mac (M1/M2/M3/M4), Windows (x64/ARM), and Linux (x64/ARM64)
documents.summary: This document provides a compatibility list detailing which CPU and GPU types are supported for running LM Studio across various operating systems, including Mac, Windows, and Linux.

documents.title: Tokenization
documents.description: Tokenize text using a model's tokenizer
documents.summary: This document details how to use the LM Studio SDK's tokenizer functionality to convert text into token IDs and demonstrates methods for counting tokens or checking if a conversation fits within a model's context window.

documents.title: Welcome to LM Studio Docs!
documents.description: Learn how to run Llama, DeepSeek, Qwen, Phi, and other LLMs locally with LM Studio.
documents.summary: This document serves as a comprehensive overview of LM Studio, detailing what it can do with local LLMs, its system requirements, how to run different types of models, and providing information on advanced features like RAG, headless operation, and accessing the built-in API.

documents.title: Offline Operation
documents.description: LM Studio can operate entirely offline, just make sure to get some model files first.
documents.summary: This document explains which core functions within LM Studio can operate entirely offline using locally downloaded models, and conversely, details the specific operations that require an active internet connection.

documents.title: Frequently Asked Questions
documents.description: Answers to common questions about LM Link.
documents.summary: This Q&A document addresses common questions about LM Link, explaining its secure operation via end-to-end encryption, compatibility with remote models and existing VPNs, and how to manage its settings and device connections.

documents.title: Manage chats
documents.description: Manage conversation threads with LLMs
documents.summary: This document provides a guide to managing conversations within LM Studio, covering features like creating chats and folders, duplicating content, utilizing split view, and explaining where the chat data is stored.

documents.title: Get started with LM Studio
documents.description: Download and run Large Language Models like Qwen, Mistral, Gemma, or gpt-oss in LM Studio.
documents.summary: This document provides a step-by-step guide on setting up and using a locally run Large Language Model (LLM) with LM Studio. It covers initial setup, downloading the model weights, loading the model into memory, and finally starting a chat session.

documents.title: LM Link
documents.description: Use LM Link to access your local models wherever you are, over a secure and end-to-end encrypted connection.
documents.summary: This document introduces LM Link, a feature in LM Studio that establishes custom, end-to-end encrypted networks to allow users to access and serve local LLMs across multiple personal devices.

documents.title: Add a Device
documents.description: Connect a new device to your LM Link.
documents.summary: This document explains how to connect and manage multiple AI devices using LM Link, detailing steps for both GUI-equipped and headless machines. It also covers loading models from remote devices via the model loader or API/SDK.

documents.title: Claude Code
documents.description: Use Claude Code with LM Studio
documents.summary: This guide explains how to configure Claude Code to interact with a local LLM server running in LM Studio using the Anthropic-compatible API endpoint.

documents.title: Using LM Link with Integrations
documents.description: Use a remote device's model with coding tools like Claude Code and Codex via LM Link
documents.summary: This document explains how to use LM Link to allow coding tools on a local machine to run language models hosted on a remote device, detailing the necessary setup for API routing.

documents.title: Integrations
documents.description: Use LM Studio with external tools and apps.
documents.summary: This document explains how to use LM Studio as a local, private backend for various development tools and integrations.

documents.title: Codex
documents.description: Use Codex with LM Studio
documents.summary: This document explains how to connect the Codex tool to a local Large Language Model (LLM) instance running via LM Studio using its OpenAI-compatible API endpoint.

documents.title: Set a preferred device
documents.description: Choose a preferred device to load models via LM Link
documents.summary: This document explains how to set a preferred remote device for accessing models via LM Link, detailing separate procedures for GUI-based and terminal-based machines.

documents.title: `lms push` (Beta)
documents.description: Upload the current folder's artifact to LM Studio Hub (beta).
documents.summary: This document explains the command-line process and various flags required to publish a new revision of a plugin, preset, or model configuration within the LMS ecosystem.

documents.title: `lms login`
documents.description: Authenticate with LM Studio Hub (beta).
documents.summary: This document details the methods for authenticating a Command Line Interface (CLI) connection with the LM Studio Hub, providing options for both interactive browser sign-in and using pre-authenticated key pairs suitable for CI/CD environments.

documents.title: `lms runtime`
documents.description: Manage LM Studio inference runtimes from the CLI.
documents.summary: This document provides a set of commands for using the `lms runtime` tool, detailing how to list, download, select, remove, and update inference runtimes.

documents.title: `lms clone`
documents.description: Clone an artifact from LM Studio Hub to a local folder (beta).
documents.summary: This document explains the usage of the `lms clone` command, which is used to copy an artifact from LM Studio Hub to a local machine.

documents.title: `lms link set-device-name`
documents.description: Rename this device on LM Link from the CLI.
documents.summary: This document explains how to use the `lms link set-device-name` command to change and set a visible display name for the current device within an LMS link.

documents.title: `lms link status`
documents.description: Check LM Link connection status and see connected peers.
documents.summary: This document explains the functionality of the `lms link status` command, which reports the operational status of LM Link on a device, including connected peers and their loaded models.

documents.title: `lms link enable`
documents.description: Enable LM Link on this device from the CLI.
documents.summary: This document explains how to manage the LM Link feature using its command-line interface (CLI), covering enabling, checking status, and disabling the connection.

documents.title: `lms link set-preferred-device`
documents.description: Set the preferred device for model resolution on LM Link.
documents.summary: This document explains how to use the `lms link set-preferred-device` command to designate a specific device as the primary source when an AI model is accessible through multiple connected devices.

documents.title: `lms daemon update`
documents.description: Update llmster to the latest version.
documents.summary: This document explains the procedure for updating the lms daemon to fetch and install either the latest stable or beta release of llmster.

documents.title: `lms daemon status`
documents.description: Check whether llmster is running.
documents.summary: This document explains how to check the operational status of the lms daemon using the `lms daemon status` command, and details related commands for starting or stopping the service.

documents.title: `lms log stream`
documents.description: Stream logs from LM Studio. Useful for debugging prompts sent to the model.
documents.summary: This document explains how to use the `lms log stream` command to inspect the exact communication strings between LM Studio and language models, useful for debugging various operations.

documents.title: `lms link disable`
documents.description: Disable LM Link on this device from the CLI.
documents.summary: This documentation provides a command-line interface (CLI) reference for managing various aspects of LM Studio, including local model operations, server management, daemon control, and the enabling or disabling of the LM Link feature.

documents.title: `lms server stop`
documents.description: Stop the running LM Studio server instance.
documents.summary: This document explains how to gracefully stop a running LM Studio server using the dedicated command.

documents.title: `lms dev` (Beta)
documents.description: Start a plugin dev server or install a local plugin (beta).
documents.summary: This document explains how to use the `lms dev` command within a plugin project to start a local development server that automatically rebuilds and reloads upon file changes for LM Studio plugins.

documents.title: `lms import`
documents.description: Import a local model file into your LM Studio models directory.
documents.summary: This document explains the usage and various flags available for the `lms import` command, detailing how to bring an existing model file into LM Studio under different conditions.

documents.title: `lms server status`
documents.description: Check the status of your running LM Studio server instance.
documents.summary: This document explains the usage and available flags for the `lms server status` command, detailing how to check the local server's operational status in various formats.

documents.title: `lms ps`
documents.description: Show information about currently loaded models from the command line.
documents.summary: This document explains how the `lms ps` command can display detailed information about all locally loaded models or connect to and query a remote LM Studio instance.

documents.title: Setup a link
documents.description: Provision your first link
documents.summary: This document explains how to begin using LM Link, a feature designed to create a network connection between various user devices within the LM Studio application.

documents.title: `lms server start`
documents.description: Start the LM Studio local server with customizable port and logging options.
documents.summary: This document details how to use the `lms server start` command to launch a local model inference server, providing instructions on setting custom ports and enabling Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) support.

documents.title: `lms ls`
documents.description: List all downloaded models in your LM Studio installation.
documents.summary: This document explains the usage of the `lms ls` command, detailing how it lists downloaded models along with various filtering options like model type and output format.

documents.title: `lms chat`
documents.description: Start a chat session with a local model from the command line.
documents.summary: This document details the various command-line flags and subcommands available with `lms chat`, allowing users to interact with local language models for single prompts, interactive sessions, or piping input.

documents.title: `lms get`
documents.description: Search and download models from the command line.
documents.summary: This document explains how to use the `lms get` command to search for and download various language models from online repositories, detailing available flags and usage options.

documents.title: Get Model Info
documents.description: Get information about the model
documents.summary: This document demonstrates how to retrieve detailed information about a loaded local language model using the getInfo method from the LMStudio SDK.

documents.title: `lms load`
documents.description: Load or unload models, set context length, GPU offload, TTL, or estimate memory usage without loading.
documents.summary: This document serves as a comprehensive guide detailing how to use the `lms load` command to manage model loading in memory, covering various optional parameters like context length and GPU offloading. It also explains how to unload models using `lms unload`.

documents.title: `lms` — LM Studio's CLI
documents.description: Get starting with the lms command line utility.
documents.summary: This document serves as a guide for users on installing, verifying, and using the `lms` command-line interface to interact with local language models.

documents.title: Contributing
documents.description: Learn where to file issues and how to contribute to the lms CLI.
documents.summary: This document directs users on how to contribute to the open-source lms CLI by listing methods for reporting bugs, requesting features, or submitting code changes.

documents.title: `lms daemon up`
documents.description: Start llmster from the CLI.

documents.title: Manage Models in Memory

documents.title: `LLMLoadModelConfig`
documents.description: API Reference for LLMLoadModelConfig
documents.summary: This document details various optional configuration parameters for controlling model behavior during inference, covering topics like GPU usage, context window management, positional embedding adjustment, and memory optimization techniques.

documents.title: List Local Models
documents.description: APIs to list the available models in a given local environment
documents.summary: This document explains how to use the `listLocalModels` method within the `LMStudioClient` object to programmatically list all models available on a local machine. The output format mimics the command-line tool's listing.

documents.title: Embedding
documents.description: Generate text embeddings from input text

documents.title: List Loaded Models
documents.description: Query which models are currently loaded

documents.title: Sharing Plugins
documents.description: How to publish your LM Studio plugins so they can be used by others
documents.summary: This document explains the process of publishing a local LM Studio plugin to the LM Studio Hub, detailing commands for packaging and uploading, as well as instructions on how to rename plugins or publish them under an organization's ownership.

documents.title: Tokenization
documents.description: Tokenize text using a model's tokenizer
documents.summary: This document explains how to use an SDK to interact with a loaded LLM or embedding model for tokenization tasks. It provides methods for both getting the full list of tokens and simply counting the total number of tokens, including checking if a conversation fits within the model's context.

documents.title: `config.ts` File

documents.title: Download an LLM

documents.summary: This document guides users on how to discover, search for, and download various AI models within LM Studio from Hugging Face, explaining the concept of model quantization when selecting a file format.

documents.summary: This code demonstrates how to access both plugin-specific and global configurations using the PreprocessorController within a TypeScript environment.

documents.title: Use MCP Servers

documents.summary: This document explains that LM Studio now functions as a Model Context Protocol (MCP) Host, allowing users to connect local or remote MCP servers; however, it strongly cautions users about the security risks associated with installing untrusted servers.

documents.title: `LLMPredictionConfigInput`
documents.summary: This document provides a detailed reference of various optional parameters used for controlling and tuning large language model generation, covering aspects like token limits, sampling diversity, context management, and structured output.

documents.title: `Add to LM Studio` Button
documents.description: Add MCP servers to LM Studio using a deeplink
documents.summary: This document explains how users can generate a deeplink to one-click install custom Model Control Panel (MCP) servers directly into LM Studio, detailing the required JSON format and URL parameters.

documents.title: Introduction to `model.yaml`
documents.description: Describe models with the cross-platform model.yaml specification.

documents.title: Get Context Length
documents.description: API to get the maximum context length of a model.
documents.summary: This document explains the concept of maximum context length for LLMs and embedding models, detailing how checking this limit is crucial before submitting long inputs to prevent erratic behavior.

documents.title: Importing and Sharing
documents.description: You can import preset files directly from disk, or pull presets made by others via URL.

documents.description: Query which models are currently loaded
documents.summary: This document demonstrates how to programmatically list models that are currently loaded into the system's memory using the SDK.

documents.title: Publish Your Presets
documents.description: Publish your Presets to the LM Studio Hub. Share your Presets with the community or with your colleagues.
documents.summary: This document guides users through the process of publishing their saved Presets from LM Studio to the community hub, allowing them to share models with other users.

documents.title: Config Presets
documents.description: Save your system prompts and other parameters as Presets for easy reuse across chats.
documents.summary: This document explains what Presets are—bundles of system prompts and parameters for reuse—and details how to manage them, including importing from URLs or files, publishing them, and where they are stored on different operating systems.

documents.title: Publish a `model.yaml`
documents.description: Upload your model definition to the LM Studio Hub.
documents.summary: This document guides users through the process of sharing portable language models by uploading a metadata file to the LM Studio Hub, detailing steps like cloning existing models, updating publisher information, authenticating via CLI, and finally publishing the model.

documents.title: Push New Revisions
documents.description: Publish new revisions of your Presets to the LM Studio Hub.
documents.summary: This document describes a preview feature that allows users to publish and share their custom Presets with the LM Studio community, detailing the steps necessary to update shared presets.

documents.summary: This document explains the process for updating locally stored or imported AI presets by pulling the latest revisions.

documents.title: Parallel Requests
documents.description: Enable parallel requests via continuous batching
documents.summary: This document explains how to enable concurrent processing of multiple model requests using continuous batching, allowing for higher throughput when loading models with llama.cpp.

documents.title: Defining New Fields
documents.summary: This document outlines the structure and usage of various supported field types, including string, numeric, boolean, stringArray, and select, detailing their configuration options for form creation.

documents.title: Speculative Decoding
documents.description: Speed up generation with a draft model
documents.summary: This document explains speculative decoding, a technique that boosts LLM generation speed by using a smaller, faster draft model to quickly propose tokens which a larger main model then verifies for accuracy.

documents.title: LM Studio in your language
documents.description: LM Studio is available in English, Chinese, Spanish, French, German, Korean, Russian, and 26+ more languages.
documents.summary: This document informs the user about the extensive multilingual support available in LM Studio and provides detailed instructions on how to change the application's language within the Settings tab.

documents.title: Import Models
documents.description: Use model files you've downloaded outside of LM Studio
documents.summary: This document explains how to import external GGUF models into LM Studio using the experimental `lms import` command and details the required directory structure for managing downloaded models.

documents.title: Prompt Template
documents.description: Optionally set or modify the model's prompt template
documents.summary: This guide explains how users can override and customize the prompt template settings for any model within LM Studio, detailing options like using Jinja templating or manual configuration.

documents.title: LM Studio Developer Docs
documents.description: Build with LM Studio's local APIs and SDKs — TypeScript, Python, REST, and OpenAI and Anthropic-compatible endpoints.
documents.summary: This document provides a comprehensive overview of the various ways to interact with LM Studio, detailing SDKs for TypeScript and Python, REST API endpoints, and command-line tools. It also includes quick start examples for setting up connections.

documents.title: API Changelog
documents.description: Updates and changes to the LM Studio API.
documents.summary: This document provides a chronological log of updates for LM Studio, detailing new features, API endpoints (including Anthropic and native v1 REST APIs), and bug fixes related to model compatibility and tool-calling capabilities across various versions.

documents.title: Authentication
documents.description: Using API Tokens in LM Studio
documents.summary: This document explains how to enable and manage API Token authentication for the LM Studio server, detailing steps for creating tokens, configuring their permissions, and demonstrating usage across REST API, Python SDK, and TypeScript SDK.

documents.title: Color Themes
documents.description: Customize LM Studio's color theme
documents.summary: This document explains two ways to access and select a theme within the application, allowing users to choose an automatic switching option.

documents.title: Run LM Studio as a service (headless)
documents.description: GUI-less operation of LM Studio: run in the background, start on machine login, and load models on demand
documents.summary: This document explains two methods for running LM Studio as a background service—the recommended standalone daemon, llmster, or using the desktop app in headless mode—and details advanced features like Just-In-Time (JIT) model loading.

documents.title: Using LM Link
documents.description: Use a remote device's model via the REST API with LM Link
documents.summary: This document explains how LM Link allows users to interact with remote models as if they were loaded locally via the REST API and SDK, maintaining normal local usage patterns.

documents.title: Per-model Defaults
documents.description: You can set default settings for each model in LM Studio
documents.summary: This document explains how to configure default loading parameters for specific models within LM Studio by accessing settings in the My Models tab or by making and saving changes during the model loading process.

documents.title: Setup llmster as a Startup Task on Linux
documents.description: Configure llmster to run on startup using systemctl on Linux
documents.summary: This guide details the process of setting up lmstudio's headless daemon, `llmster`, to run automatically on Linux systems using systemd for service management. It covers installation, model downloading, manual testing, and creating the necessary systemd service file.

documents.title: Using MCP via API
documents.description: Learn how to use Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers with LM Studio API.
documents.summary: This document details how LM Studio supports Model Context Protocol (MCP) for enabling models to interact with external tools via standardized servers. It compares and explains the use cases for defining these servers either ephemerally per request or pre-configuring them in an mcp.json file.

documents.title: Idle TTL and Auto-Evict
documents.description: Optionally auto-unload idle models after a certain amount of time (TTL)
documents.summary: This document explains the concepts and configurations for managing model memory using Just-In-Time (JIT) loading, detailing features like Idle TTL and Auto-Evict to automatically manage when models are kept or removed from memory.

documents.title: Serve on Local Network
documents.description: Allow other devices in your network use this LM Studio API server
documents.summary: This document explains how enabling the "Serve on Local Network" option makes the LM Studio API server accessible to other devices connected to the same local network.

documents.title: LM Studio as a Local LLM API Server
documents.description: Run an LLM API server on localhost with LM Studio
documents.summary: This document explains multiple methods for serving local Large Language Models (LLMs) from LM Studio, including using the Developer tab, the command line interface, and accessing various API formats.

documents.title: LM Studio API
documents.description: LM Studio's REST API for local inference and model management
documents.summary: This document details the REST API provided by LM Studio, highlighting the official v1 endpoints and its compatibility with OpenAI and Anthropic standards. It outlines key features like stateful chats and model management.

documents.title: Stateful Chats
documents.description: Learn how to maintain conversation context across multiple requests
documents.summary: This document explains how the stateful nature of the /api/v1/chat endpoint works, detailing methods for continuing conversations using response IDs or disabling state storage entirely.

documents.title: Server Settings
documents.description: Configure server settings for LM Studio API Server
documents.summary: This document details various configurable server settings for an API, allowing users to manage networking parameters like port numbers, authentication requirements, and features such as CORS and dynamic model loading.

documents.title: User or Developer
documents.description: Show more advanced settings and developer features
documents.summary: This document explains how to enable Developer Mode within the application settings and helps users decide which operational mode—User, Developer, or Power User features—best suits their technical needs.

documents.title: Streaming events
documents.description: When you chat with a model with stream set to true, the response is sent as a stream of events using Server-Sent Events (SSE).
documents.summary: This document details the structure and various types of named events streamed via Server-Sent Events (SSE) when calling the `/api/v1/chat` endpoint for incremental chat response rendering. It covers lifecycle stages such as model loading, prompt processing, reasoning, tool calling, message streaming, and final error handling.

documents.title: Load a model
documents.description: Load an LLM or Embedding model into memory with custom configuration for inference
documents.summary: This document provides a snapshot of the loading status and configuration details for an LLM instance identified as openai/gpt-oss-20b.

documents.title: Chat with a model
documents.description: Send a message to a model and receive a response. Supports MCP integration.
documents.summary: This document details the structure and parameters for a POST request to the `/api/v1/chat` endpoint, which is used to communicate with a model for chat interactions. It covers the required request body fields, optional configuration parameters like temperature and context length, and describes the various possible structures found within the response payload.

documents.title: Download a model
documents.description: Download LLMs and embedding models
documents.summary: This document describes the API endpoint for initiating and checking the status of a model download, detailing required request body parameters and possible response fields.

documents.title: Get download status

documents.summary: This document details the structure and parameters for a GET request endpoint used to check the current status of a background download job.

documents.title: REST API v0

documents.description: Send requests to Responses, Chat Completions (text and images), Completions, and Embeddings endpoints.

It outlines the high-level flow, from setting up the server to programmatically passing tool definitions to the model and then feeding the results back into the conversation context.

documents.title: Configuring the Model
documents.description: APIs for setting inference-time and load-time parameters for your model
documents.summary: This document explains how to configure a model by detailing both inference-time parameters, which are set per request (like temperature and maxTokens), and load-time parameters, which are set when initializing the model instance.

documents.title: Working with Chats
documents.description: APIs for representing a chat conversation with an LLM
documents.summary: This document explains the different methods for supplying chat input parameters to SDK methods like llm.respond(), detailing options including using a single string for simple chats, utilizing the recommended Chat helper class for complexity, and passing chat history data directly as a dictionary.

documents.title: The `.act()` call
documents.description: How to use the .act() call to turn LLMs into autonomous agents that can perform tasks on your local machine.
documents.summary: This document explains the concept of 'execution rounds' in LLM tool use, detailing the cycle of running a tool, providing results to the model, and waiting for the next decision. It also covers advanced topics like parallel tool calls, model selection best practices, and various progress callbacks available during complex agent interactions.

documents.title: Tool Definition
documents.description: Define tools to be called by the LLM, and pass them to the model in the act() call.
documents.summary: This document explains how to define and use tools with language models by passing Python functions or `ToolFunctionDef` objects. It also details how error handling for tool calls is managed by default and through custom callbacks.

documents.title: Unload a model
documents.description: Unload a loaded model from memory
documents.summary: This documentation details the API endpoint used to programmatically unload a loaded model instance from memory.

documents.title: Chat with Documents
documents.description: How to provide local documents to an LLM as additional context
documents.summary: This document explains how attaching document files in LM Studio provides context to large language models, detailing when it uses full context versus Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

documents.title: `lms daemon down`
documents.description: Stop llmster from the CLI.
documents.summary: This document explains the purpose and usage of the `lms daemon down` command, specifying that it is used to stop a running llmster daemon but will not affect an actively running GUI instance.

documents.title: Manage Models in Memory
documents.description: APIs to load, access, and unload models from memory
documents.summary: This document details how to programmatically manage large AI models using LM Studio's SDK, covering methods for loading, accessing specific instances, managing namespaces, and safely unloading models from memory.

documents.title: Get Context Length
documents.description: API to get the maximum context length of a model.
documents.summary: This document explains the concept of context length in LLMs and embedding models, detailing why adhering to this limit is crucial for stable operation.

documents.title: Get Load Config
documents.description: Get the load configuration of the model
documents.summary: This document explains how to retrieve the configuration parameters used when loading a specific model using the LM Studio Python SDK.

documents.title: List Downloaded Models
documents.description: APIs to list the available models in a given local environment
documents.summary: This document explains how to iterate through locally available models and use the provided listing results to obtain full SDK handles for loaded model instances.

documents.title: Embedding
documents.description: Generate text embeddings from input text
documents.summary: This document explains that embeddings are vector representations capturing semantic meaning, which serves as a foundational component for tasks like RAG and similarity searches, detailing how to obtain an embedding model and subsequently generate the required vectors.

documents.title: `lmstudio-js` (TypeScript SDK)
documents.description: Getting started with LM Studio's Typescript / JavaScript SDK
documents.summary: This document introduces an SDK that provides programmatic tools for interacting with Large Language Models (LLMs), embeddings models, and agentic workflows, detailing installation instructions and key features.

documents.title: Authentication
documents.description: Using API Tokens in LM Studio
documents.summary: This document explains how to securely authenticate access to the LM Studio API using API Tokens, detailing the recommended method of setting an environment variable or passing it as a function argument.

documents.title: Cancelling Predictions
documents.description: Stop an ongoing prediction in lmstudio-python
documents.summary: This document explains how using a streaming API allows developers to cancel a prediction request based on custom, application-specific conditions beyond standard configuration limits.

documents.title: Chat Completions
documents.description: APIs for a multi-turn chat conversations with an LLM
documents.summary: This document details various methods for interacting with an LLM through the `llm` object, covering obtaining a model handle, managing conversation context, generating responses, customizing inference parameters, and printing prediction statistics.

documents.title: Text Completions
documents.description: Provide a string input for the model to complete
documents.summary: This document details how to use the `llm.complete()` function to generate text completions from a loaded language model. It covers steps for instantiating the model, generating predictions, and accessing prediction metadata, including options for customizing parameters and implementing progress callbacks.

documents.title: Image Input
documents.description: API for passing images as input to the model
documents.summary: This document provides a step-by-step guide on how to use Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by first ensuring a VLM is available, then preparing an input image using specific client methods, and finally passing both the image and prompt to the model's respond method.

documents.title: Structured Response
documents.description: Enforce a structured response from the model using Pydantic (Python), Zod (TypeScript), or JSON Schema
documents.summary: This document explains methods for forcing a language model to generate responses conforming to a specific structure, detailing the use of both Zod and standard JSON schemas.

documents.title: Speculative Decoding
documents.description: API to use a draft model in speculative decoding in lmstudio-js
documents.summary: This document explains how to implement speculative decoding within the `lmstudio-js` library to boost LLM generation speed while maintaining response quality.

documents.title: Project Setup
documents.description: Set up your lmstudio-js app or script.
documents.summary: This document provides instructions on how to integrate and set up the @lmstudio/sdk library into new or existing Node.js projects.

documents.title: Working with Chats
documents.description: APIs for representing a chat conversation with an LLM
documents.summary: This document outlines three different methods for passing chat context when calling SDK methods, including using an array of messages, providing a single string, or employing the recommended `Chat` helper class.

documents.title: Generate Completions
documents.description: Provide a string input for the model to complete
documents.summary: This document explains the sequence of steps required to generate text completions using a loaded language model, covering model instantiation and prediction statistics retrieval.

documents.title: The `.act()` call
documents.description: How to use the .act() call to turn LLMs into autonomous agents that can perform tasks on your local machine.
documents.summary: This document introduces the concept of 'execution rounds' to describe the iterative process where an LLM runs tools, receives the output, and then decides on subsequent actions until a final result is achieved.

documents.title: Configuring the Model
documents.description: APIs for setting inference-time and load-time parameters for your model
documents.summary: This document explains how to customize model behavior by detailing two types of parameters: inference-time settings, which are set per request for controlling output generation, and load-time settings, which configure the model when it is initially loaded.

documents.title: Using `npm` Dependencies
documents.description: How to use npm packages in LM Studio plugins
documents.summary: This document explains how to manage and install necessary npm dependencies for developing plugins compatible with LM Studio, advising developers to use 'npm' exclusively.

documents.title: Cancelling Predictions
documents.description: Stop an ongoing prediction in lmstudio-js
documents.summary: This document explains two methods for halting a running text prediction process using lmstudio-js: calling `.cancel()` on the prediction object or utilizing an existing `AbortController` signal.

documents.title: Tool Definition
documents.description: Define tools with the tool() function and pass them to the model in the act() call.
documents.summary: This document explains how to define and pass tools to a model, detailing that these tools can execute external effects like file creation or API calls.

documents.title: Multiple Tools
documents.summary: This code snippet demonstrates how to define and register custom, asynchronous tooling functions within a provided controller for an AI system.

documents.title: Introduction to Tools Provider
documents.description: Writing tools providers for LM Studio plugins using TypeScript
documents.summary: This document explains the concept and purpose of writing tools providers for LM Studio plugins using TypeScript, detailing how these providers equip large language models with external functionalities.

documents.title: Status Reports & Warnings
documents.summary: This document explains how to provide status updates and warnings during tool execution, and also details how to gracefully handle prediction aborts using the AbortSignal object.

documents.title: Custom Configuration
documents.description: Add custom configuration options to your tools provider
documents.summary: This code provides a function to define and return an array of available tools for a controller, specifically demonstrating how to implement a file creation tool using local file system operations.

documents.title: Introduction
documents.description: Writing prompt preprocessors for LM Studio plugins using TypeScript
documents.summary: This document explains the function and mechanism of Prompt Preprocessors, detailing how they intercept and modify user input before it reaches a language model, especially when multiple preprocessors are chained.

documents.title: Examples
documents.summary: This document provides an example of a prompt preprocessor designed to manipulate incoming chat messages by replacing specific trigger words with custom instructional text.

documents.title: Custom Configuration
documents.summary: This code snippet demonstrates how to define and build configuration schematics for an SDK, specifically adding fields for 'Special Instructions' and a 'Trigger Word'.

documents.title: Introduction to Plugins
documents.description: A brief introduction to making plugins for LM Studio using TypeScript.
documents.summary: This document guides developers on how to build and manage plugins for LM Studio, detailing the necessary setup, development workflow, and advanced features like tools providers and prompt preprocessors.

documents.title: Handling Aborts
documents.summary: This document informs developers that they must gracefully handle an abortion event by checking for and responding to the `ctl.abortSignal` when a user cancels a running prediction generation.

documents.title: Custom Status Report
documents.summary: This document demonstrates how to manage and report the processing status of a prompt preprocessor using `ctl.setStatus` and updating it with different states, including sub-statuses.

documents.title: Text-only Generators
documents.summary: This document explains how generators function within a controller context, detailing methods for reporting generated text and covering specific operational aspects like accessing custom configurations and handling user abort signals.

documents.title: Handling Aborts
documents.description: Gracefully handle user-aborted tool executions in your tools provider
documents.summary: This code demonstrates how to define and register custom tools, specifically a 'fetch' tool, within an SDK controller.

documents.title: Introduction
documents.description: Add custom configurations to LM Studio plugins using TypeScript
documents.summary: This document explains that LM Studio plugins can support custom configurations by defining a schema, allowing users to configure the plugin through a UI without code changes. It details the types of configurations available (per-chat vs. global) and the supported TypeScript field types.

documents.title: Introduction
documents.description: Writing generators for LM Studio plugins using TypeScript
documents.summary: This document explains that generators serve as substitutes for local LLMs, directing text generation through external or remote sources instead of the locally loaded model.

documents.title: Single Tool
documents.summary: This document explains the necessary steps for setting up a tools provider within a plugin, detailing how to define a specific tool like 'create_file' and then register that provider in the plugin's main entry file.

documents.title: Tool calling generators
documents.summary: This document outlines the specific sequence of control methods (ctl) that a generator must call when handling tool use generation, detailing steps for reporting text fragments and managing both successful and failed tool call generations.
